title: A method for estimating width bands of variables in economics under uncertainty conditions
author: Ganjoei, Akbarifard, et al.
description: This study develops a method to estimate the width bands of variables in economics by fuzzy logic. One of its important features is flexibility in the conditions of economic uncertainty, which can be used to model the uncertainty of external and internal factors on economic variables. In this study, for example, the effect of uncertainty of external factors on the Gini coefficient (income distribution) is investigated. For this purpose, we use the fuzzy logistic smooth transition autoregressive (FLSTAR) model and the Gini coefficient is estimated in three bounds (high, middle and low). The result of this estimation suggest that by appropriate policy making the Gini coefficient can be decreased to the lower bound. Another results of this study is that the authorities should prevent the increase of the Gini coefficient in the middle and upper bands with proper planning for the future. In brief,â€¢This study introduces a novel method for estimating high, low and middle bounds of economic variables under uncertainty conditions.â€¢One practical results of this method is to compare high, medium, and low bands of the variables with their current trends, which is a benchmark for policymaking and evaluating the effectiveness of government's policies.â€¢Programs designed with this method are fast and have low cost

steps_raw.title: Fuzzy logic methodology
steps_raw.instruction: Fuzzy sets were introduced by L. A. Zadeh [7]. After introducing this notion, the fuzzy data were used for modeling uncertain information in the databases. Fuzzy logic involves a wide range of theories and techniques that are generally based on four concepts: fuzzy sets, verbal variables, membership function, and fuzzy if-then rules [8,9]. The fuzzy logic consists of three stages as fuzzification, fuzzy process (fuzzy inference) and defuzzification [10]. In this Section, we will review the application of fuzzy logic in prediction and modeling.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: The main focus in this section is on modeling the nonlinear behavior of the variables in economics. For this purpose EViews software is used [16]. EViews can be used for general statistical analysis and econometric analyses, such as cross-section, panel data analysis and time series estimation, and forecasting. The EViews software handles nonlinear time series modeling. The input variables include: degree of economic openness (OPEN), foreign direct investment (FDI), and integration of international trade (IIT). In this study, the nonlinear LSTAR model is used to evaluate the behavior of the variables. In order to select the appropriate nonlinear model (LSTAR) in EViews software, we use Taylor's third-degree expansion, Lagrange multiplier (LM)-type tests and testing hypothesis, respectively [17], [18], [19], [20]. The steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models are as follows

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 0 - stable variables: In the time series, the augmented Dickey Fuller test is the test trying to analyze whether the variables have unit roots or not. In 1974, Granger and Newbold found that there could have been a spurious regression problem if the variables are not stable.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: ADF unit root test regression equation;خ”Yt=(دپâˆ’1)Ytâˆ’1+ut=خ´Ytâˆ’1+utwhere خ” denotes the first difference value of the variable. The model is estimated as a result of the analysis and then the hypothesis H0: خ´= 0 is applied to the variables. Since the variables in the serie are connected to a random variable, the H0 hypothesis can be interpreted that the unit root exists, that is, the variables are non-stationary. As the residual values are used in the ADF unit root test, t statistic with a special table is used instead of the standard t statistic value [21].

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction:
Step 1 - Testing linearity: One of the most important steps in estimating a smooth transfer regression model is to test the linear model against the nonlinear. The main question is that if the model is nonlinear, which process (ESTAR or LSTAR model) does it follow? Therefore, the null hypothesis based on the linear is defined as H0=0,v>0
[9,17]. In Eq.آ (14) when v=0, the LSTAR model reduces to a linear regression [18,20]:(14)G(st;v,c)=(1+exp{âˆ’v(stâˆ’c)})âˆ’1,v>0.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: In this case, the parameters St and c are indefinite. The solution that Luukkonen etآ al. (1988) [17] and Terأ¤svirta (1994) [7] have proposed to solve this problem is to replace the transfer function with Taylor's approximation. After replacing the transition function by its third-order Taylor approximation around vآ =آ 0, the auxiliary regression model is obtained. Taylor's third-degree expansion is based on the suggestion made by Luukkonen etآ al. (1988) [17]. steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: We use Akaike, Bayesian or Schwarz, and Hannan-Quinn criteria (henceforth AIC, BIC, and HQ) for determine the optimal lag. According to this criterion, the optimal lag for the variables is one. Tableآ 1 presents the results of criteria AIC, BIC, and HQ.Table 1Optimal lag determination.Tableآ 1:The number of lagCriteriaAICBICHQ0âˆ’3.615âˆ’4.019âˆ’3.5271âˆ’4.718*âˆ’4.834*âˆ’5.2302âˆ’3.507âˆ’3.516âˆ’4.5403âˆ’4.315âˆ’3.314âˆ’3.517Note: Computing Akaike, Bayesian (AIC) or Schwarz (BIC), and Hannan-Quinn (HQ) criteria for the optimal lag.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 2 - Choice of the transition variable: In order to select the transfer variable, according to Taylor's first to third approximations, LMW statistic [20] is calculated for each of the independent variables. The variable that has the highest LMW test statistic among the other variables is selected as the transfer variable [19]. For further reading see [19,24].

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 3 - Choice of the transition function: If the model is non-linear, the appropriate form must be selected for the transfer function. For this purpose, the following hypotheses are tested in order:H1:خ²â€²3=0H2:خ²â€²2=0|خ²â€²3=0H3:خ²â€²1=0|خ²â€²2=0,خ²â€²3=0where | the term is conditional.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: If the H1 hypothesis is rejected, the model will have the LSTAR pattern, and if the H1 hypothesis is accepted, the H2 hypothesis is tested. If this hypothesis is rejected, the model will have the ESTAR model, otherwise, the hypothesis H3will be tested. IfH3 is rejected, the model will have the LSTAR model.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 4 - Transition velocity v and transition pointci are determined by using the Newton-Raphson algorithm [21]. Transfer point c and transfer velocity point v, are calculated based on Eq.آ (28), where خ±i, iآ =آ 0,1,â€¦,4 and خ²i, iآ =آ 0,1,â€¦,4; also, خ±i and خ²i are real coefficients of the linear and non-linear parts of the model (29), respectively, the estimates of which are provided in Appendix, Table 13, p.31. GinitisGinicoefficient, FDI is foreign direct investment, IIT is integration of international trade and OPEN is the degree of economic openness and Ginitâˆ’1 is Gini coefficient of the previous period. Thus, we guess the initial approximations of these two points to achieve the convergence among them (estimation results are in Appendix).(29)Gini=خ±0+خ±1Ginitâˆ’1+خ±2FDItâˆ’1+خ±3IITtâˆ’1+خ±4OPENtâˆ’1+(خ²0+خ²1Ginitâˆ’1+خ²2FDItâˆ’1+خ²3IITtâˆ’1+خ²4OPENtâˆ’1)*(1+exp{âˆ’v(OPENtâˆ’1âˆ’c)})âˆ’1+خµt,

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 5 - The threshold values of the transition function are calculated based on the following relationshipG(st;v,c)=(1+exp{âˆ’v(stâˆ’c)})âˆ’1,v>0.

steps_raw.title: Steps of the modeling cycle for the methodology LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: The LSTAR model as a regime-switching model can include two thresholds. The amount of thresholds depends on the amount of transition function, so that the value of the transfer function can have values 0 and 1. Parameter c can be interpreted as the thresholds between two regimes. The threshold value in the LSTAR model is related to small and large absolute values of st (again relative to c). See [20,24,25].

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Today, fuzzy systems are extensively used in various sciences. In this study, MATLAB software [12], is used present the manner of to design a fuzzy model. To enter the fuzzy environment in MATLAB software, we use the fuzzy command. Then the following page appears.

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: On this page, there are input membership functions (input1), output membership functions (output 2), and inference engine (Mamdani) Fig.آ 2. Fuzzy rules are used in inference engine for creating the acceptable answer. In the previous steps (1â€“5) the LSTAR modeling method was described. In Step 6 the LSTAR estimation method is expressed using fuzzy logic.Fig.آ 2Input membership functions (input1), output membership functions (output2), and inference engine (Mamdani).Fig 2:

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 6 - The modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models is as follows. The construction of the fuzzy inference system consists of four parts, which are shown in Fig.آ 3. For more information.Fig.آ 3Main components of a fuzzy inference system [10].Fig 3

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: A-Fuzzifier: The first step in creating a fuzzy system is to define inputs and membership functions. Membership functions should be structured in such a way that they clearly convey the meanings of the relevant linguistic words. In this study, the input and output membership functions is of the Gaussian type written as follows:خ¼A(x)=expâˆ’(xâˆ’c)22دƒ2,where x input value دƒ is the standard deviation and c is the mean inputs. Fig.آ 4-C, D, E, F and G represents the input membership functions. Fig.آ 4-H represents the output membership function (as presented in Appendix).Fig.آ 4C: membership functions FDI, D: membership functions IIT, E: membership functions OPEN, F: membership functions TR G: Gini coefficient, H: membership functions Gini coefficient with one lag Gini t-1.Fig 4

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: B-Rule Base: Fuzzy rule base is a phrase with an if-then structure. The number of rules required depends on the number of indicators and the number of classes in each indicator and is calculated according to the Iآ =آ m tob power n, where m is the number of status and n number of input.

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Fuzzy rules have two parts: "if part" and "then part"; they are mainly expressed as the following statements:ifx1isA1randx2isA2randxnisAnrthenisBr,Air are fuzzy sets such as large, medium, positive large, and r represents the rth rule. B is the output of the system. In this study, fuzzy rules are written based on Tableآ 2. The representation of the rules in MATLAB software is shown in Fig.آ 5 (as presented in Appendix).Table 2Inference rules of FIS.Tableآ 2:RulesIFTHENFDIIITOPENGINIt-1TRGini coefficientDegree1HighHighHighmiddleLowHigh12HighHighmiddleLowLowHigh13HighHighLowmiddleLowHigh14middleHighHighHighLowMiddle15middleHighmiddleLowLowMiddle16middleHighLowmiddleLowMiddle17LowHighHighLowLowMiddle18LowHighmiddleHighLowMiddle19LowHighLowLowLowLow1.......................243LowLowLowmiddlemiddleLow1Note: Fuzzy rules based on input variables included: foreign direct investment, degree of economic openness, integration of international trade and output variable Gini coefficient.Fig.آ 5Rule representation of the proposed FIS.Fig 5

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: C-Inference engine: Inference engine is a component of the system that applies logical rules to the knowledge base in order to deduce new information.

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: D-Defuzzification: In this section, the fuzzy output obtained in the previous section is converted into a non-fuzzy number. There are five common ways to do this: centroid, bisector, middle of maximum, smallest of maximum, and largest of maximum.

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: In this study, the centroid method is used in the defuzzification, and the irregularity is covered by the desired fuzzy number. The centroid method is as follows:output=âˆ«x.خ¼(x)dxâˆ«خ¼(x)dx,where خ¼ is the fuzzy number membership function of the input.

steps_raw.title: Modeling cycle for the methodology FRBS in the framework of LSTAR models
steps_raw.instruction: Step 7 -In order to estimate the Gini coefficient of high, middle and low bands in accordance with (30), the inputs are initially analyzed according to the fuzzy-rules base [11] as in (3). The transfer function consists of three parameters of cثœ, vثœ, and Sثœt.(30)Giniثœi,t(Giniثœtâˆ’j,FDIثœ,OPENثœ,IIT,ثœSثœit,c,ثœvثœ)=âˆ‘j=1nخ±jGiniثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jFDIثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jOPENثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jIITثœtâˆ’j+Gثœ(Sثœit,vثœ,cثœ)*{âˆ‘j=1nخ±jGiniثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jFDIثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jOPENثœtâˆ’j+âˆ‘j=1nخ±jIITثœtâˆ’j}Gثœ(Sثœit,vثœ,cثœ)=11+exp(vثœ(Sثœitâˆ’cثœ))where iآ =آ 1, 2, â€¦ , N, where Nآ =آ 3 and N is number of width bands, tآ =آ 1, 2, â€¦ , T, where T is the last year studied, namely 2017, jآ =آ 1, 2, â€¦ , n, where nآ =آ 20, and n is the range of the study period (1997â€“2017),where Gini coefficient Giniثœ, the effect of foreign trade as the degree of economic openness (OPEN),(Ginitâˆ’1) Gini coefficient of the previous period, foreign direct investment (FDI, integration of international trade (IIT), transition variable Sثœ, transition point cثœ, transition velocity vثœ all of which are fuzzified. Note that ~ means the notion used is fuzzified.
